startScreenOptions.title: AN18.1-AN18.7 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 2 embedded questions for AN18.1-AN18.7.

interactions.label: Why does a fracture at the neck of the fibula commonly result in foot drop?

params.question: Why does a fracture at the neck of the fibula commonly result in foot drop?

answers.text: It damages the superficial peroneal nerve, paralysing the foot evertors

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The superficial peroneal nerve supplies the lateral compartment muscles (peroneus longus and brevis) for eversion, not dorsiflexion. Foot drop involves loss of dorsiflexion, which is an anterior compartment function.

answers.text: It injures the deep peroneal nerve, which innervates all four dorsiflexors of the foot

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The deep peroneal nerve (L4, L5) winds around the fibular neck and supplies tibialis anterior, extensor hallucis longus, extensor digitorum longus, and peroneus tertius — all four dorsiflexors. Injury here abolishes dorsiflexion, producing foot drop.

answers.text: It compresses the anterior tibial artery, causing ischaemia of the anterior compartment

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: While the anterior tibial artery runs through the anterior compartment, foot drop is an isolated motor deficit from nerve injury, not arterial ischaemia. Vascular compromise would cause broader signs including pain, pallor, and pulselessness.

answers.text: It ruptures the tibial nerve, paralysing the plantarflexor muscles

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The tibial nerve runs through the posterior compartment and supplies the plantarflexors (gastrocnemius, soleus). Its injury would impair plantarflexion and toe-off during gait, not dorsiflexion.

overallFeedback.feedback: The deep peroneal nerve (L4, L5) winds around the neck of the fibula, where it is vulnerable to fractures. It innervates all four anterior compartment muscles — tibialis anterior, EHL, EDL, and peroneus tertius — which all dorsiflex the foot. Loss of these muscles causes foot drop.

Correct! The deep peroneal nerve is superficial and vulnerable as it winds around the fibular neck. Since it supplies all four anterior compartment dorsiflexors, injury here causes foot drop with a characteristic steppage gait.

interactions.label: In the unhappy triad, the medial meniscus tears along with the medial collateral

params.question: In the unhappy triad, the medial meniscus tears along with the medial collateral ligament because:

answers.text: The medial meniscus is thinner and structurally weaker than the lateral meniscus

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The vulnerability of the medial meniscus is not due to its thickness. It is C-shaped and robust. Its susceptibility in the unhappy triad comes from being physically tethered to the MCL.

answers.text: The valgus force directly compresses the medial meniscus between the femoral and tibial condyles

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: A valgus force opens the medial side of the knee (separation, not compression). The medial meniscus tears because it is physically attached to the MCL and is pulled apart when the ligament ruptures.

answers.text: The medial meniscus is firmly attached to the MCL, so MCL disruption drags and tears the meniscus with it

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The medial meniscus is anchored to the medial collateral ligament. When a valgus force tears the MCL, the tethered meniscus is pulled apart. The lateral meniscus, being unattached to the LCL, escapes this mechanism and moves freely.

answers.text: The medial meniscus has a poorer blood supply, making it more susceptible to traumatic tearing

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: While the inner two-thirds of both menisci have limited vascularity, the reason the medial meniscus tears specifically in the unhappy triad is its firm attachment to the MCL. Blood supply does not explain this particular injury pattern.

overallFeedback.feedback: The medial meniscus is firmly attached to the medial collateral ligament, unlike the lateral meniscus which is free from the LCL. In the unhappy triad, a lateral blow tears the MCL first, which drags and tears the attached medial meniscus, and then the ACL gives way.

Well done! The medial meniscus is tethered to the MCL — this structural connection is why it tears in the unhappy triad. The lateral meniscus, being free from the LCL, is spared by the same mechanism.
